era_context: Second Intermediate Period in Egypt; Kassite Babylon in Mesopotamia; Shang dynasty in China; Middle Minoan III period in the Aegean. This era is characterized by political fragmentation, the rise of chariot warfare, and the consolidation of new dynasties across the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East.

events.title: Possible Thera volcanic eruption (Minoan eruption)
events.description: The Thera eruption, one of the largest volcanic events in recorded history, may have occurred around this time, though the dating is highly disputed. The eruption devastated the Minoan civilization on Crete and surrounding islands, causing tsunamis, ash fall, and climate disruption. The eruption may have inspired later Greek myths about Atlantis. However, the precise date of the eruption is contested, with scholarly estimates ranging from 1650 to 1450 BCE, a span of 200 years. Some scholars place it earlier (c. 1650 BCE), others in the mid-17th century, and still others in the 15th century.

sources.name: Geological and stratigraphic evidence from Thera and Crete
Radiocarbon dating of volcanic ash and organic material
Egyptian records (possible reference in Ahmose Papyrus, though disputed)

events.certainty_note: The Thera eruption is geologically confirmed, but its precise date is highly uncertain. Radiocarbon dating gives a range of 1650–1450 BCE (with a peak probability around 1600 BCE), but this range is too broad to assign to a specific year. The assignment to 1636 BCE is speculative and reflects one possible interpretation of the evidence.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Thera eruption is one of the most contested issues in Mediterranean archaeology. Recent radiocarbon studies suggest a date around 1600 BCE (±50 years), but earlier estimates placed it as late as 1450 BCE. Some scholars argue that the eruption had less impact on Minoan civilization than traditionally believed, and that the decline of Minoan culture was a gradual process rather than a sudden catastrophe. The connection between the Thera eruption and the Greek myth of Atlantis is speculative and not supported by direct evidence.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1636 BCE is minimal. The only potentially significant event—the Thera eruption—is geologically confirmed but archaeologically and chronologically uncertain. The dating of the eruption relies on radiocarbon analysis, which provides a probability distribution rather than a precise date. The assignment of the eruption to this specific year is speculative. The impact of the eruption on Minoan civilization is debated; some scholars argue it was catastrophic, while others suggest it was one of several factors in the gradual decline of Minoan culture. No written records from this year survive from any civilization.

graph_edges.note: The Thera eruption (if it occurred around this time) may have contributed to the decline of Minoan civilization, though the relationship is debated
